title: Qatayef
subtitle: Arabic stuffed pancakes with ricotta and pistachios, fried until golden
excerpt: Qatayef are small leavened pancakes cooked on one side only, then filled with sweet ricotta and pistachios, sealed into a half-moon, and fried until crisp. Soaked briefly in rose-water syrup before serving. They are a Ramadan staple across the Arab world, though the recipe is good any time of year.
sourceNotes: Qatayef (also spelled katayef, qatayef, atayef) are a pan-Arab sweet with documented roots in the Abbasid period. They are eaten primarily during Ramadan across Egypt, Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, and the Gulf states. The pancakes are leavened with yeast or baking powder, cooked on one side so the top surface remains tacky enough to seal. The filling varies by region: cheese, cream, or nuts. This recipe follows the Levantine sweet ricotta and pistachio tradition with a rose-water syrup.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter can be made up to 2 hours ahead. The syrup can be made a week ahead. Fry and serve on the day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: cooked on a dry flat pan (no oil), then deep-fried at 170 degrees C

glossaryTerms.definition: Qatayef pancakes are cooked on one side only. The uncooked top surface should remain slightly tacky and covered in small bubbles that have opened but not dried. This sticky surface is what allows the edges to seal when pressed together. If the top dries out completely, the pancake will not seal and will open during frying. Remove from the pan as soon as the bubbles have mostly opened and the underside is golden.

content.text: The key to qatayef is the

technique that leaves the top surface tacky enough to seal. Once sealed, the filled qatayef fry quickly and the syrup soaking takes only seconds. Serve warm, not hot, so the ricotta has time to firm slightly.

Whisk together 250 g plain flour, 1 tsp baking powder, 1 tbsp caster sugar, and 3 g salt. Gradually whisk in 350 ml warm milk until smooth. Rest for 20 minutes. Meanwhile, make the syrup: dissolve 200 g caster sugar in 150 ml water in a small saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a boil and cook for 3 minutes. Remove from heat and stir in 2 tsp rose water. Set aside. Mix the filling: combine 250 g ricotta, 3 tbsp icing sugar, 1 tsp orange blossom water, and 50 g finely chopped pistachios.

Heat a dry flat pan over medium-low heat. Pour 2 tablespoons of batter per pancake. Cook until the top is covered in open bubbles and the underside is golden, about 2 minutes. Do not flip. Remove and place cooked-side down on a clean surface. Place a heaped teaspoon of filling in the centre of each pancake. Fold in half and press the edges firmly together to seal. Make sure there are no gaps.

Heat 750 ml vegetable oil to 170 degrees C. Fry the filled qatayef in batches for 2 to 3 minutes, turning once, until deep golden and crisp. Remove with a spider strainer. While still hot, dip briefly into the rose-water syrup, about 10 seconds per side, and arrange on a plate. Scatter with any remaining pistachios.

items.solution: The edges were not sealed firmly enough, or the top surface had dried out before sealing. Press the edges firmly with fingertips all the way around. If the top has dried too much, the pancake will not seal; add a little water to dampen the edge before pressing.

items.problem: Pancakes are thick and stodgy rather than light
items.solution: The batter was too thick. It should be pourable, similar to thin crepe batter. Add a little extra milk. The batter also needs the full 20-minute rest for the leavening to activate.
